headline: Last Chance Gulch: Helena Founded on a Gold Strike, July 14, 1864
summary: On July 14, 1864, four prospectors known as the 'Four Georgians' struck gold at Last Chance Gulch in the foothills of Helena. The strike ignited one of Montana's richest gold rushes and the camp that grew up around it became the city of Helena.

headline: Montana's Capital Since 1875: Helena Beat Anaconda in Two Bitter Elections
summary: Helena became Montana Territory's capital in 1875 and, after two bitterly contested elections, was confirmed as the state capital in 1894 — defeating Anaconda by roughly 2,000 votes in a campaign financed by copper-king rivals William A. Clark and Marcus Daly.

headline: Millionaire Row: Helena Once Had More Millionaires Per Capita Than Anywhere on Earth
summary: By 1888 Helena was home to roughly 50 millionaires — more per capita than any other city in the world at the time. The fortunes were built on the gold of Last Chance Gulch and the silver and lead mines of the surrounding ranges.

headline: Kalispell Founded 1891: Charles Conrad and the Great Northern Railway
summary: Kalispell was founded in 1891 when fur trader and businessman Charles E. Conrad bought a 400-acre townsite at a planned division point on James J. Hill's new Great Northern Railway. Conrad's vision and the railroad together set the future Flathead County seat in motion.

headline: Named for Le Havre: How Havre Got Its French Name in 1893
summary: Havre was incorporated in 1893 and named at the suggestion of two French Canadian merchants, after the port city of Le Havre, France. The town grew around a Great Northern Railway division point on James J. Hill's transcontinental line.

headline: Havre Beneath the Streets: An Underground City Born from the 1904 Fire
summary: After a devastating 1904 fire destroyed much of downtown Havre, merchants reopened beneath the burned streets, operating saloons, opium dens, restaurants, and shops in a network of tunnels. The Havre Beneath the Streets museum still leads tours through the preserved underground rooms.

headline: Wahkpa Chu'gn: A 2,000-Year-Old Bison Jump Behind the Havre Holiday Village
summary: Wahkpa Chu'gn is a buffalo jump archaeological site on the Milk River bluffs just behind Havre, used by Indigenous peoples for over 2,000 years. Excavated layers reveal centuries of bison hunts; the site is open for guided tours from the Holiday Village Mall.

headline: Stumptown's Roots: Whitefish Incorporated 1905 After Great Northern Arrival
summary: Whitefish — known locally as Stumptown for the giant tree stumps left after the area was cleared for the Great Northern Railway — was incorporated as a city on April 21, 1905. The railroad's arrival in 1904 turned a logging camp into a permanent town.

headline: Kootenai Nation: 14,000 Years of History Before Whitefish Existed
summary: Long before Whitefish was Stumptown, the Kootenai (Ksanka) people lived in the Flathead Valley for at least 14,000 years. Their fishing, hunting, and trading routes crossed the lake and rivers that European settlers later named for whitefish, the species the Kootenai had harvested for millennia.

headline: 1966 Great Northern Buelow Collision: Whitefish's Worst Rail Accident
summary: On April 23, 1966, two Great Northern freight trains collided head-on near Buelow Siding outside Whitefish, killing four crew members. The wreck became one of the deadliest rail accidents in northwestern Montana history and reshaped local railroad operating rules.

headline: Laurel Founded 1882: From Railroad Section House to a City of 7,000
summary: Laurel began in 1882 as a Northern Pacific Railway section house at the mouth of the Clarks Fork River. Originally called Carlton, the town was renamed Laurel by a railroad official after the abundant laurel shrubs in his North Carolina hometown.

headline: From Carlton to Laurel: How a Local Shrub Renamed a Montana Railroad Town
summary: The original Northern Pacific townsite at the mouth of the Clarks Fork was called Carlton. When a railroad official noticed the laurel shrubs growing wild around the station and recalled his North Carolina home, he successfully petitioned to rename the town Laurel — the name it has carried since 1909.

headline: Clarks Fork Camp: Where William Clark Stopped in 1806
summary: On July 24, 1806, Captain William Clark of the Lewis & Clark Expedition camped at the confluence of the Clarks Fork and Yellowstone Rivers — the spot that would become Laurel. Clark's journals mark the location as the eastern entrance to the Yellowstone valley.

headline: Fort Missoula: The 1877 Army Post That Anchored the Missoula Valley
summary: Fort Missoula was established in 1877 to protect settlers in western Montana during the Nez Perce War. It later trained the 25th Infantry Bicycle Corps (an all-Black regiment that bicycled 1,900 miles to St. Louis in 1897) and served as an internment camp during World War II.

headline: Hellgate Treaty 1855: The Agreement That Opened the Missoula Valley
summary: Signed July 16, 1855 at Hellgate (near present-day Missoula), the treaty between the U.S. government and the Bitterroot Salish, Pend d'Oreille, and Kootenai nations established the Flathead Reservation and opened the surrounding lands to settlement. The treaty's contested ratification still shapes tribal rights in the region.

headline: Higgins Bridge: 1881 Span That Connected Missoula's Two Halves
summary: Capt. Christopher Higgins's first bridge across the Clark Fork was completed in 1881, finally linking the north and south halves of the new town of Missoula. The current Higgins Avenue Bridge, the third on the site, was built in 1962 and remains the city's primary downtown crossing.

headline: Northern Pacific Reached Missoula in 1883: How the Railroad Built a City
summary: On July 4, 1883, the Northern Pacific Railway connected Missoula to the rest of the country, just months before the line was completed across the continent. The arrival of the railroad transformed Missoula from a frontier outpost into the commercial heart of western Montana.

headline: Jeannette Rankin: Missoula's Trailblazing First Woman in U.S. Congress
summary: Born near Missoula in 1880, Jeannette Rankin became the first woman elected to the U.S. Congress in 1916 — four years before American women won the right to vote nationally. She was the only member of Congress to vote against U.S. entry into both World Wars.

headline: Frank Little: Butte Labor Organizer Lynched During the 1917 Strike
summary: Industrial Workers of the World organizer Frank Little came to Butte in July 1917 to support striking copper miners. On August 1, six masked men dragged him from his boarding-house room and lynched him from a railroad trestle — a killing that still goes unsolved and that helped galvanize Montana's labor movement.
